steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The claim: Toomer uses figurative language to connect the narrator's urban DC environment with the rural South of the narrator's past. The right quote must (1) use figurative language and (2) explicitly link both settings.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — “The [train] engines of this valley have a whistle, the echoes of which sound like iterated gasps and sobs. I always think of them as crude music.”: Describes train sounds — no figurative link between DC and the South. ✗
  • Choice B — “I sang, with a strange quiver in my voice, a promise-song.”: Mentions singing but connects no two settings and lacks DC/South imagery. ✗
  • Choice C — “The young trees had not outgrown their [planter] boxes then. V Street [in Washington, DC] was lined with them.”: Only describes DC street trees; the rural South never appears. ✗
  • Choice D — “And when the wind is from the South, soil of my homeland falls like a fertile shower upon the lean streets of [Washington, DC].”: The simile "like a fertile shower" is figurative language; "soil of my homeland" (South) falling on DC streets fuses both settings in one image. ✓
That confirms D is correct.
